title.en: Abolition of satī — Rammohan Roy and Regulation XVII

dating.display: campaign c. 1818–1829; Bengal Regulation XVII: 4 December 1829
dating.basis: Fully documented in print: Roy's tracts (1818, 1820), the Regulation text (1829), and the Privy Council appeal record (upheld 1832).

summary: Rammohan Roy's public campaign argued from within the tradition's own texts that widow immolation lacked śāstric sanction — deploying Ṛgvedic funeral verses and the anti-satī positions in the dharmaśāstra debate — while colonial statistics documented the practice in Bengal. Governor-General Bentinck's Regulation XVII declared satī illegal and punishable by the criminal courts; orthodox petitioners appealed to the Privy Council and lost (1832).
whyItMatters: The atlas's clearest example of the tradition's capacity for argued self-reform: the decisive public case was made in the śāstras' own terms by a Hindu reformer, not imposed as pure external fiat. It is also the historical anchor for the ledger's satī entry — both the reality of the practice and the reality of internal opposition.
honestyNote: Agency was genuinely dual: Roy's internal textual campaign AND the colonial state's legislative power. Crediting either alone distorts. The practice also did not vanish by decree — isolated cases recurred (Roop Kanwar, Rajasthan, 1987, prompting India's Commission of Sati (Prevention) Act, 1987).

locations.name: Calcutta (Kolkata), West Bengal, India — seat of the Regulation

sources.label: Bengal Regulation XVII of 1829 (text in parliamentary papers)
Rammohan Roy, Conference between an Advocate for, and an Opponent of, the Practice of Burning Widows Alive (1818; second tract 1820)
Mani, L., Contentious Traditions: The Debate on Sati in Colonial India (California, 1998)
Kane, P.V., History of Dharmaśāstra, Vol. II — satī section and the śāstric debate

image.alt: Abolition of satī — Rammohan Roy and Regulation XVII

dharmicTie.statement: The decisive argument was scriptural: Roy's case stood on the Ṛgvedic funeral hymn itself — which sends the widow back to the living — and on the anti-satī jurists within dharmaśāstra. The tradition's own texts ended the practice's textual defense.

scriptureRefs.text: udīrṣva nāri — 'rise, woman, to the world of the living' — the funeral hymn's actual instruction
the jurists' condemnation Brick's study documents
